questions.q: What is the difference between symmetric and asymmetric encryption, and where is each used?

answer.oneLiner: Symmetric encryption uses one shared key for both locking and unlocking data - it is fast and used for bulk data. Asymmetric encryption uses a public key to encrypt and a private key to decrypt - it is slower and used for key exchange and digital signatures.
answer.detail: Symmetric encryption, like AES-256, uses the same key to encrypt and decrypt. Both sides must have the same secret key, which creates a key-distribution problem - how do you share the key safely the first time? This is why it is used after a secure channel is established. Asymmetric encryption, like RSA or ECC, uses a key pair: the public key is shared openly and encrypts data, while the private key is kept secret and decrypts it. Anyone can lock a message, but only the owner can unlock it. Asymmetric is 100 to 1000 times slower than symmetric, so it is not practical for large data. In practice TLS combines both: asymmetric to authenticate the server and securely exchange a session key, then symmetric (AES) for all the actual data transfer.
keyPoints: Symmetric (AES) uses one shared key - fast, used for data at rest and bulk transfers
Asymmetric (RSA/ECC) uses a public/private pair - slower, used for key exchange and digital signatures
TLS handshake uses asymmetric to agree on a shared AES session key, then switches to symmetric for speed
Symmetric's weakness is safely distributing the shared key to both parties
Never use asymmetric encryption for bulk data - it is impractical and slow

code.note: RSA encrypts the AES key (small), and AES encrypts the report data (large) - this is the hybrid pattern used in TLS and PGP.

answer.analogy: Asymmetric is the padlock, symmetric is the fast conveyor belt inside the warehouse.
redFlags: Saying asymmetric encryption replaces symmetric for encrypting bulk data at rest or in transit
Not knowing that TLS uses both types - asymmetric for the handshake and symmetric for data transfer
Confusing encryption with hashing when asked about storing passwords
Claiming RSA can encrypt payloads of any size without mentioning its hard byte limit

followUpsQA.q: Why does TLS use both symmetric and asymmetric encryption?
followUpsQA.a: Asymmetric handles the handshake to authenticate and safely exchange a session key, then symmetric AES handles all data transfer because it is much faster.

followUpsQA.q: Why can you not just use RSA to encrypt everything?
followUpsQA.a: RSA is 100 to 1000 times slower than AES and has a hard size limit per operation - for 2048-bit RSA that is about 214 bytes, making it unusable for bulk data.

followUpsQA.q: What is the main weakness of symmetric encryption?
followUpsQA.a: Key distribution - both parties must securely share the same secret key before communication starts, which is hard to do safely.

followUpsQA.q: Which algorithm would you use to encrypt a SQL Server database at rest?
followUpsQA.a: AES-256 via Transparent Data Encryption - it is symmetric, fast, and designed for bulk storage encryption.

followUpsQA.q: What does a digital signature use and why?
followUpsQA.a: Asymmetric encryption - the sender signs with their private key and anyone with the public key can verify the signature, proving both identity and integrity.

dialogue.text: Can you explain the difference between symmetric and asymmetric encryption, and where you would use each?

Symmetric uses one shared key for both encrypting and decrypting - think AES-256, which is fast and used for bulk data like files or database records. Asymmetric uses a public key to encrypt and a private key to decrypt - think RSA, which is slower but solves the key distribution problem because the public key can be shared openly.
So which one does TLS use when my browser connects to a banking site?

Both, in sequence. The TLS handshake uses asymmetric encryption to authenticate the server and negotiate a shared session key. After that, all the actual data travels encrypted with AES because symmetric is orders of magnitude faster for bulk traffic.
Why not just use RSA for everything and skip the complexity of two algorithms?

RSA has a hard size limit - for a 2048-bit key you can only encrypt about 214 bytes per operation. It is also 100 to 1000 times slower than AES, so encrypting a 10 MB customer report with RSA alone would either fail or be painfully slow.
In C# code, how would you implement the hybrid pattern you described?

Generate a random AES key, encrypt the payload with AES, then encrypt only the AES key with RSA. The recipient uses their RSA private key to recover the AES key, then decrypts the payload. This is exactly what PGP and TLS 1.2 do under the hood.
What about key management for the AES key itself in an Azure-hosted app?

Store the AES key in Azure Key Vault, accessed via Managed Identity so there is no stored credential. Key Vault also handles key rotation and audit logs for every access, which satisfies compliance requirements.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

questions.q: What's the difference between hashing and encryption?

answer.oneLiner: Encryption is a two-way process - you lock data with a key and unlock it with a key. Hashing is a one-way process - you turn data into a fixed fingerprint that cannot be reversed.
answer.detail: Encryption protects data you need to read back later. You encrypt a credit card number so only your payment service can decrypt it. Hashing is for verification, not recovery. You hash a password and store the hash. On login, you hash what the user types and compare - you never need to see the original password. The key difference is intent: encryption is for confidentiality of data you must retrieve, hashing is for integrity and verification where the original value must never be stored. A common confusion is encoding, like base64, which is neither - it is a reversible format change with no key and provides zero security. Another trap is using a fast hash like SHA-256 for passwords - SHA-256 is designed to be fast for checksums, which lets attackers brute-force billions of guesses per second. For passwords you need a slow adaptive hash like bcrypt or Argon2.
keyPoints: Encryption is reversible with a key - used for confidentiality of data you need back
Hashing is one-way with no key - used for password storage and integrity checks
Base64 encoding is neither - it is a format change with no security properties at all
Use slow adaptive hashes (bcrypt, Argon2) for passwords, not fast hashes like SHA-256 or MD5
You verify a hash by hashing the input again and comparing - you never decrypt a password

code.note: Encrypting passwords is wrong because decryption is possible - a breach exposes all original passwords. Hashing is one-way so a breach only leaks the hash.

answer.analogy: Encryption is a lockbox - use the key to get your valuables back. Hashing is a blender - nothing comes back out.
redFlags: Saying hashing is just encryption you cannot undo - they are fundamentally different concepts with different purposes
Confusing base64 encoding with encryption - encoding provides zero security
Recommending SHA-256 or MD5 for password storage - these are too fast for password hashing
Not knowing that passwords should be hashed and never encrypted or stored in plain text

followUpsQA.q: When would you use encryption versus hashing?
followUpsQA.a: Use encryption for data you must retrieve later, like a stored credit card number. Use hashing for data you only need to verify, like a password.

followUpsQA.q: Is base64 a form of encryption?
followUpsQA.a: No - base64 is a reversible encoding format with no key and no security. Any tool can decode it instantly.

followUpsQA.q: Why is SHA-256 bad for passwords but fine for file checksums?
followUpsQA.a: SHA-256 is designed to be very fast, which is good for checksums but lets attackers try billions of password guesses per second. Passwords need slow adaptive hashes like bcrypt.

followUpsQA.q: How do you verify a password if you never store it?
followUpsQA.a: On login you hash the submitted password using the same algorithm and salt, then compare the resulting hash to the stored hash.

followUpsQA.q: What does a salt do in password hashing?
followUpsQA.a: A random salt added before hashing ensures two users with the same password get different hashes, defeating rainbow table attacks.

dialogue.text: Can you explain the difference between hashing and encryption?

Encryption is two-way - you use a key to lock data and the same or a paired key to unlock it, because you need to read the data back. Hashing is one-way - you run data through a function to get a fixed-length fingerprint and there is no way to reverse it.
So should I encrypt passwords or hash them?

Hash them - you never need to read a password back, you only need to verify it at login. If you encrypt passwords, a database breach combined with a key leak exposes all original passwords. With hashing, even if the database is stolen, the hashes cannot be reversed.
Can I just hash with SHA-256? It is fast and widely available.

Fast is exactly the problem for passwords. SHA-256 can process billions of hashes per second on a GPU, so an attacker can brute-force a weak password in seconds. For passwords I use bcrypt or Argon2 with a work factor that makes each guess take around 100ms.
A junior developer says they are base64-encoding sensitive config values for security. Your reaction?

Base64 is encoding, not encryption - it has no key and any tool decodes it in one line. It provides zero security. Those secrets belong in Azure Key Vault or environment variables injected at runtime.
How does password verification work if you never store the original password?

The stored record contains the hash and the salt. On login, I take the submitted password, apply the same algorithm with the same salt, and compare the output hash to the stored one. bcrypt embeds the salt in the hash string, so BCrypt.Verify handles all of that automatically.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

questions.q: How should you store user passwords?

answer.oneLiner: Store a slow, salted hash using an adaptive algorithm like bcrypt, PBKDF2, or Argon2 - never store the plain password or use a fast hash like MD5 or SHA-256.
answer.detail: When a user sets a password, you generate a random salt unique to that user, combine it with the password, and run the result through a slow adaptive hash algorithm. Slow means it takes tens or hundreds of milliseconds per attempt, which is fine for one login but makes brute-force attacks take years. Adaptive means you can increase the work factor as hardware gets faster. The output contains the algorithm, work factor, salt, and hash in one string, so you store only that. On login, you hash the submitted password with the stored salt and compare. You never see or store the original password at any point. ASP.NET Core Identity does all of this for you using PBKDF2 by default. The most common mistakes are using MD5 or SHA-1 (which are fast and unsuitable for this purpose), forgetting to salt (which allows rainbow table attacks), or using the same salt for all users.
keyPoints: Use a slow adaptive algorithm: bcrypt, Argon2, or PBKDF2 - never MD5, SHA-1, or plain SHA-256
Each password must have its own unique random salt to prevent rainbow table attacks
The work factor should make each attempt take 100ms to 300ms - increase it as hardware improves
ASP.NET Core Identity handles correct password hashing automatically with PBKDF2
On login, hash the submitted password with the stored salt and compare hashes - never compare plain text

code.note: bcrypt embeds the salt and work factor in the stored string - no separate salt column needed. Work factor 12 means 2^12 iterations per attempt.

answer.analogy: A fast hash is a screen door - a slow adaptive hash is a bank vault with a dial that takes 10 seconds to turn.
redFlags: Saying MD5 or SHA-256 is acceptable for passwords - these are designed to be fast, not to be slow password hashes
Not knowing what a salt is or why every user needs their own unique salt
Suggesting storing passwords in any reversible form, even encrypted
Not knowing that ASP.NET Core Identity handles this correctly out of the box

followUpsQA.q: Why is a fast hash like SHA-256 a problem for passwords?
followUpsQA.a: SHA-256 can compute billions of hashes per second on a GPU, so an attacker with a leaked database can brute-force common passwords in seconds.

followUpsQA.q: What is the purpose of a salt?
followUpsQA.a: A unique random salt per user ensures that two users with the same password produce different hashes, and it defeats precomputed rainbow table lookups.

followUpsQA.q: Does ASP.NET Core Identity handle this correctly?
followUpsQA.a: Yes - it uses PBKDF2 with HMAC-SHA512, a random salt, and a high iteration count. Use Identity rather than rolling your own.

followUpsQA.q: How often should you increase the work factor?
followUpsQA.a: Review it as hardware gets faster - target 100ms to 300ms per hash on your server, and increment the factor when it drops below that.

followUpsQA.q: What happens if you use the same salt for all users?
followUpsQA.a: Two users with the same password produce the same hash, letting an attacker identify shared passwords and build a targeted rainbow table for your specific salt.

dialogue.text: How should you store user passwords in a .NET application?

Never store the plain password. Hash it with a slow adaptive algorithm like bcrypt, Argon2, or PBKDF2, using a unique random salt per user. The stored value is only the hash output, which is not reversible.
Why not just use SHA-256 - it is a strong cryptographic hash?

SHA-256 is designed to be fast, which is great for checksums but terrible for passwords. A modern GPU can compute billions of SHA-256 hashes per second, so brute-forcing a weak password takes seconds. bcrypt with work factor 12 makes each attempt take around 100ms by design.
Two of our customers use the same password. Will their stored hashes match?

No - each user gets their own randomly generated salt before hashing. So even with identical passwords, the hash outputs are completely different. This also defeats rainbow table attacks because precomputed tables cannot account for unique per-user salts.
Do you need to implement all of this yourself in a new ASP.NET Core app?

No - ASP.NET Core Identity handles it correctly with PBKDF2 and HMAC-SHA512 by default. I would use Identity rather than rolling my own, since password hashing is one of those areas where subtle mistakes have serious consequences.
What do you do when hardware gets faster and your work factor becomes too weak?

Increase the work factor in config and rehash passwords on the next successful login - you have the submitted plain password at that point. Users who never log in can be prompted to reset, or you can run a background migration.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

questions.q: How do you manage application secrets and connection strings?

answer.oneLiner: Keep secrets completely out of source control - use Azure Key Vault accessed via Managed Identity in production, and dotnet user-secrets for local development, injecting values at runtime.
answer.detail: The rule is simple: a secret that touches a file in your repository is a compromised secret, even in a private repo. In production, store secrets in Azure Key Vault and let the app access it using Managed Identity, which means the app has no stored credential of its own - the Azure platform handles authentication. In code, DefaultAzureCredential automatically uses Managed Identity in Azure and developer credentials like Visual Studio sign-in or the Azure CLI locally. For local development, dotnet user-secrets stores values in a JSON file outside the project folder (in the user profile), so they never get committed. In CI/CD pipelines, use the platform's masked secret variables, never plain environment files checked in. ASP.NET Core's configuration system layers all these sources together, so the app reads IConfiguration the same way regardless of where the value comes from.
keyPoints: Never commit secrets - not in appsettings.json, not in environment files, not anywhere in the repo
Production: Azure Key Vault with Managed Identity - no stored credential needed to access secrets
Local development: dotnet user-secrets stores values outside the project folder
DefaultAzureCredential handles both environments transparently in the same code
CI/CD: use masked secret variables in the pipeline, never commit plain .env files

code.note: DefaultAzureCredential uses Managed Identity in Azure and developer CLI/VS credentials locally - no code change between environments.

answer.analogy: A secret in your repo is a key under the doormat - everyone who can clone the repo has it.
redFlags: Saying secrets in a private repo are safe - private repos get breached, forked, and cloned by CI runners
Storing secrets in appsettings.json or environment files that are committed to git
Not knowing what dotnet user-secrets is or where it stores values on disk
Confusing Managed Identity with a service principal that has a stored password

followUpsQA.q: How does the app read Key Vault secrets without having its own stored credential?
followUpsQA.a: Managed Identity - the Azure platform issues and rotates a token for the app automatically, so DefaultAzureCredential uses it with no secret in code or config.

followUpsQA.q: Where does dotnet user-secrets actually store the values?
followUpsQA.a: In a JSON file under the user's profile directory outside the project folder, so it cannot be committed to the repository.

followUpsQA.q: How do you prevent secrets from appearing in CI/CD logs?
followUpsQA.a: Use masked secret variables in the pipeline tool - GitHub Actions secrets or Azure DevOps secret variables are redacted from all log output automatically.

followUpsQA.q: What does DefaultAzureCredential try in order?
followUpsQA.a: It tries several credential types in sequence: environment variables, Managed Identity, Visual Studio, Azure CLI, and others - so the same code works in Azure and locally.

followUpsQA.q: A junior committed an API key to the repo 3 commits ago. What do you do?
followUpsQA.a: Rotate or revoke the key immediately - assume it is compromised. Then purge it from git history with git filter-repo and add secret scanning to prevent recurrence.

dialogue.text: How do you manage secrets and connection strings in a .NET application?

The core rule is: no secret ever touches the repository. In production I use Azure Key Vault accessed via Managed Identity - the app has no stored credential of its own. For local development I use dotnet user-secrets, which stores values outside the project folder so they cannot be committed.
How does the app in production authenticate to Key Vault if it has no stored credential?

Managed Identity - the Azure platform issues a token for the app automatically and rotates it. In code I use DefaultAzureCredential, which picks up the Managed Identity token in Azure and falls back to my developer credentials locally, so the same code works in both environments.
Show me what that looks like in startup code.

builder.Configuration.AddAzureKeyVault(new Uri(vaultUrl), new DefaultAzureCredential()). That one line adds Key Vault as a configuration source, so the app reads secrets through the standard IConfiguration API with no special Key Vault code in business logic.
What about secrets in the CI/CD pipeline for things like container registry credentials?

Use the pipeline platform's native secret variables - GitHub Actions secrets or Azure DevOps secret variables. These are masked in all log output and injected as environment variables at runtime. I never commit a .env file or plain config file with credentials.
Someone on your team says the private repo makes it safe to put the connection string in appsettings.json. How do you respond?

Private repos get breached, forked, and cloned - and anyone with repo access at any point in history has the secret. The safe assumption is that anything committed is potentially exposed. Key Vault with Managed Identity removes the secret from the repo entirely.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

questions.q: What's the difference between protecting data at rest and in transit?

answer.oneLiner: In transit means encrypting data moving over the network using TLS or HTTPS. At rest means encrypting data stored on disk, in a database, or in blob storage.
answer.detail: These two protections guard against different threats. In-transit encryption (TLS 1.2+) protects data from being intercepted while it travels between a client and a server, or between two services. Without it, anyone on the network path can read the data in plaintext. At-rest encryption protects data from being read if physical media or storage is accessed without authorization - for example, a stolen database backup or a decommissioned disk drive. SQL Server's Transparent Data Encryption (TDE) encrypts the database files on disk automatically. Azure Storage encrypts blobs at rest by default. Important: at-rest encryption does not stop SQL injection, because the database decrypts data transparently for any valid query, including an injected one. You need both protections because they cover entirely different attack scenarios. Keys for both should be managed in a Key Management Service like Azure Key Vault, not hardcoded.
keyPoints: In transit: TLS 1.2+ for all HTTP traffic, mTLS for internal service-to-service calls
At rest: Transparent Data Encryption for databases, storage-level encryption for blobs and disks
They protect against different threats - interception versus physical media theft
At-rest encryption does not protect against SQL injection because the DB decrypts for valid queries
Manage encryption keys in Key Vault - never hardcode them in application code

code.note: In-transit encryption covers the network hop. At-rest encryption (TDE, storage encryption) covers the storage layer - you need both independently.

answer.analogy: In-transit encryption is an armored truck - at-rest encryption is a locked vault at the destination.
redFlags: Assuming HTTPS also protects data stored in the database - these are completely separate protections
Not knowing what TDE (Transparent Data Encryption) is for SQL Server databases
Thinking at-rest encryption prevents SQL injection - the DB decrypts transparently for all valid queries
Not mentioning that both protections are needed because they cover different threats

followUpsQA.q: Does at-rest encryption stop SQL injection attacks?
followUpsQA.a: No - the database decrypts data transparently for any valid query, including a malicious injected one. Parameterized queries prevent SQL injection, not encryption.

followUpsQA.q: What is Transparent Data Encryption?
followUpsQA.a: TDE encrypts the physical database files on disk automatically. If someone steals the disk or a backup file, the data is unreadable without the encryption key.

followUpsQA.q: What is mTLS and when do you use it?
followUpsQA.a: Mutual TLS is where both client and server present certificates to authenticate each other - used for service-to-service calls inside a cluster or microservices mesh.

followUpsQA.q: If a company uses only HTTPS, is customer data fully protected?
followUpsQA.a: No - HTTPS only protects data in transit. Stored data in the database or blobs needs separate at-rest encryption to be protected from storage-level breaches.

followUpsQA.q: Who manages the encryption keys for at-rest encryption?
followUpsQA.a: A key management service like Azure Key Vault - keys should never be hardcoded in the application or stored alongside the data they protect.

dialogue.text: Can you explain the difference between protecting data at rest versus in transit?

In transit means encrypting data as it travels over the network - we use TLS 1.2 or higher for all HTTP traffic. At rest means encrypting data that is stored somewhere - on disk, in a database file, or in blob storage. They protect against completely different threats.
What specific threats does each one protect against?

In-transit encryption stops a network attacker from reading data as it moves between the browser and the server, or between two microservices. At-rest encryption stops someone who gains physical access to a disk or a stolen database backup from reading the raw data.
Our database uses Transparent Data Encryption. Does that protect us from SQL injection?

No - TDE encrypts the physical database files on disk, but the database engine decrypts data transparently for any valid query, including a malicious injected one. SQL injection protection comes from parameterized queries, not from encryption.
We have HTTPS on the API and TDE on the database. Is there anything else to consider?

Enforce TLS 1.2 or 1.3 minimum and disable older protocols, use mTLS for internal service-to-service calls, and manage encryption keys in Key Vault rather than hardcoding them. For highly sensitive fields like payment card data, column-level encryption adds a third layer on top of TDE.
Where do you keep the encryption keys themselves?

In Azure Key Vault, accessed via Managed Identity. The key must never be stored alongside the data it protects - that is like putting a lockbox key inside the lockbox. Key Vault also provides key rotation and a full audit log of every access.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

questions.q: How do you isolate data in a multi-tenant application?

answer.oneLiner: Choose an isolation model based on your cost and security requirements: a separate database per tenant offers the strongest isolation, a shared database with a TenantId column is the cheapest, and separate schemas sit in between.
answer.detail: Each model trades isolation strength for operational cost. Database-per-tenant gives the strongest isolation - a misconfigured query cannot leak data to another tenant because the databases are physically separate. It is expensive: you need to provision, migrate, and maintain one database per customer. Schema-per-tenant shares one database server but separates the data structures per tenant, giving moderate isolation at lower cost. Shared-schema puts all tenants in the same tables and uses a TenantId column to separate rows. This is the cheapest and most common for large SaaS apps but requires strict discipline - every query must include the tenant filter. With EF Core, you enforce this centrally using a global query filter that adds WHERE TenantId = currentTenantId to every query automatically. The current tenant must always come from the authenticated user context on the server, never from a value the client sends in the request body.
keyPoints: Database-per-tenant is the strongest isolation but most expensive to operate and scale
Shared-schema is cheapest but every query must include the tenant filter - one missed WHERE clause is a data breach
Use EF Core global query filters to enforce the tenant filter centrally, not per-query
Always set the current tenant from the server-side auth context, never from a client-supplied value
Test for cross-tenant data leakage explicitly in your security test suite

code.note: The TenantId in _tenantContext must be resolved from the JWT claim server-side, never from a request parameter the client can manipulate.

answer.analogy: Shared-schema multi-tenancy is a shared office building - invisible walls only hold if every door has an automatic lock.
redFlags: Trusting a TenantId value that comes from the request body or a query parameter - clients can send any value
Relying on developers to remember to add a tenant filter to every query instead of enforcing it centrally
Not being able to name the three isolation models and their trade-offs
Not knowing that EF Core global query filters can enforce tenant scoping automatically

followUpsQA.q: What is the main risk of the shared-schema model?
followUpsQA.a: A single query missing the TenantId filter can return rows from another tenant, which is a serious data breach in a regulated environment.

followUpsQA.q: How do you get the current tenant ID safely?
followUpsQA.a: From the authenticated user's JWT claim server-side - for example, a custom tenantId claim set by the identity provider at login, never from the request body.

followUpsQA.q: What is a global query filter in EF Core?
followUpsQA.a: A LINQ predicate registered on a model entity that EF Core automatically appends to every query for that entity, so the tenant filter cannot be omitted by a developer.

followUpsQA.q: When would you choose database-per-tenant despite the cost?
followUpsQA.a: For high-compliance customers like healthcare or banking who require physical data separation, or when customers contractually require their own database for audit purposes.

followUpsQA.q: How do you run database migrations across hundreds of tenant databases?
followUpsQA.a: Automate a migration runner that iterates through all tenant connection strings and applies pending migrations as part of the deployment pipeline.

dialogue.text: We are building a SaaS invoicing product. How would you isolate data between tenants?

There are three main models. Database-per-tenant gives the strongest isolation and is simplest to reason about but costly to operate. Shared-schema with a TenantId column is cheapest and most common for large SaaS products. Schema-per-tenant sits in the middle. The right choice depends on your compliance requirements and expected tenant count.
We are going with shared-schema to keep costs down. What is the biggest risk?

A single query missing the WHERE TenantId clause returns data from all tenants - that is a data breach. The way I prevent this is with an EF Core global query filter on every multi-tenant entity, so the filter is applied automatically and a developer cannot forget it.
Show me how you would set that up.

In OnModelCreating: modelBuilder.Entity<Invoice>().HasQueryFilter(i => i.TenantId == _tenantContext.CurrentTenantId). The _tenantContext is injected and resolves the tenant ID from the authenticated user's JWT claim at the start of each request.
What stops a customer from sending a different TenantId in the request to access another tenant's invoices?

The tenant ID never comes from the request body or query string. It comes from the JWT claim that was set by the identity provider at login, which the client cannot modify without invalidating the token signature. The server resolves it from HttpContext.User.Claims.
One of your enterprise customers says their compliance policy requires physical data separation. What do you offer?

A dedicated database for that tenant. Many SaaS products support a hybrid model - most tenants share a database, while high-compliance customers get their own. The connection string lives in Key Vault and is resolved by tenant ID at startup.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

questions.q: What does handling PII and GDPR basics involve?

answer.oneLiner: Collect only the personal data you genuinely need, secure it with encryption and access controls, give users control over their own data, and notify the regulator within 72 hours of a breach.
answer.detail: GDPR (General Data Protection Regulation) applies to any EU resident's personal data, regardless of where your company is based. PII (Personally Identifiable Information) is any data that can identify a person: name, email, IP address, location, and so on. The core obligations are: collect only what you have a lawful basis for and genuinely need (data minimization and purpose limitation); secure it with encryption, strict access controls, and audit logs; give users the right to access their data, correct it, and have it deleted or anonymized on request; notify the supervisory authority within 72 hours of discovering a breach that risks individual rights. In practice this means your database design must support deletion or anonymization of a user record without breaking referential integrity, your logs must not contain raw PII (use pseudonymization or opaque IDs), and you must have an incident response plan so you can detect and report breaches in time.
keyPoints: Data minimization: collect only what you have a lawful basis for and actually need
Right to erasure: users can request deletion or anonymization of their personal data
GDPR breach notification: notify the supervisory authority within roughly 72 hours of discovery
Never log raw PII - use opaque IDs or pseudonymized data in logs and error messages
Design the data model to support user deletion without breaking the rest of the system

code.note: PII in logs creates a second data store subject to GDPR with weaker controls - use opaque IDs in logs and look up PII only when needed for a specific support case.

answer.analogy: GDPR treats personal data like borrowed money - use only what you need, return it on request, and report it missing immediately.
redFlags: Storing personal data indefinitely with no deletion or anonymization path in the data model
Logging full email addresses, IP addresses, or names in application or error logs
Not knowing what the GDPR breach notification deadline is - the answer is roughly 72 hours
Confusing pseudonymization with anonymization - pseudonymized data can still be re-linked and stays in GDPR scope

followUpsQA.q: What is the right to erasure in practice?
followUpsQA.a: When a user requests deletion, you delete or anonymize their personal data in all systems - database, backups, logs, and third-party services - within the regulatory deadline.

followUpsQA.q: What is the difference between pseudonymization and anonymization?
followUpsQA.a: Pseudonymized data replaces identifiers with tokens but can be re-linked using a key, so it is still personal data under GDPR. Anonymized data cannot be re-linked at all and leaves GDPR scope.

followUpsQA.q: What is the GDPR breach notification deadline?
followUpsQA.a: Roughly 72 hours after becoming aware of a breach that poses a risk to individual rights - you notify the supervisory authority, not necessarily the affected individuals directly.

followUpsQA.q: Why should you not log raw PII?
followUpsQA.a: Logs become a second PII store subject to GDPR rules, often with weaker access controls and longer retention periods than the main database.

followUpsQA.q: What lawful basis would an e-commerce company typically use for order data?
followUpsQA.a: Contract - processing an order requires the customer's address and payment details. Consent covers optional marketing emails. Legitimate interest can cover fraud prevention.

dialogue.text: What does handling PII and GDPR basics actually involve from a developer's perspective?

At a high level: collect only the personal data you have a lawful basis for, secure it with encryption and access controls, give users the right to access and delete their data, and have a plan to notify the regulator within 72 hours of a breach.
What does implementing the right to erasure look like in a database-backed application?

The data model has to support it. For a customer record I would either hard-delete it or replace all personal fields with anonymized values and remove the link to their identity. The tricky part is ensuring referential integrity holds - order history might keep the OrderId but the customer row gets anonymized.
A support engineer wants to add customer email addresses to the application logs for easier debugging. Is that acceptable?

No - logs become a second PII data store subject to GDPR, often with weaker access controls and longer retention than the main database. I would log only the customer ID and look up the email in the database only when needed for a specific support case.
We discover a breach on a Tuesday afternoon. When do we have to notify the regulator?

Within roughly 72 hours of becoming aware of it, so by Friday afternoon. That is why you need an incident response plan in place before a breach happens - you cannot afford to spend the first 48 hours figuring out your process.
What is the difference between pseudonymization and anonymization, and why does it matter for GDPR?

Pseudonymized data replaces identifiers with tokens but keeps a mapping table that can re-link them - it is still personal data under GDPR. Truly anonymized data cannot be re-linked to an individual at all and leaves GDPR scope entirely. Only full anonymization removes the obligation.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

questions.q: What is the principle of least privilege and how do you apply it?

answer.oneLiner: Give every user, service, and process only the minimum access it needs to do its specific job - and nothing beyond that.
answer.detail: Least privilege limits the blast radius of any security incident. If an attacker compromises a component that has only the access it needs, they can do far less damage than if that component had broad access. In practice this means: the application's database account should have only SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE on the tables it needs - not db_owner or sa. API tokens and service accounts should be scoped to the specific resources and actions they call. Azure Managed Identity should have only the RBAC roles it needs on the specific Key Vault secrets it reads, not Contributor on the whole subscription. Users should have only the roles their job requires. Access should be reviewed and revoked when it is no longer needed. The default stance should be deny - grant access explicitly rather than trying to block it after the fact. This principle also applies to secrets: a service that only reads from a storage account should have Storage Blob Data Reader, not Storage Account Contributor.
keyPoints: Database service account: only the CRUD operations it needs on specific tables, not db_owner
Azure RBAC: scope roles to the specific resource, not the subscription or resource group
Default stance is deny - grant access explicitly and review it periodically
Reduces blast radius: a compromised component with narrow access causes far less damage
Apply to tokens, service accounts, API scopes, Azure roles, and database permissions

code.note: With Managed Identity and table-level permissions, a successful SQL injection cannot drop tables, read other schemas, or run OS commands.

answer.analogy: A hotel key card opens your room and the gym - not every room on every floor.
redFlags: Connecting to production databases as sa or db_owner for convenience or to avoid granting specific permissions
Giving a service account subscription-level Contributor when it only needs to read one Key Vault secret
Not reviewing or revoking access when employees change roles or leave the company
Thinking least privilege is only about database accounts - it applies to all access including API scopes and Azure roles

followUpsQA.q: How does least privilege reduce the impact of a SQL injection attack?
followUpsQA.a: If the database account can only SELECT and INSERT on specific tables, a successful injection cannot drop tables, read other schemas, or execute xp_cmdshell to run OS commands.

followUpsQA.q: How do you apply least privilege in Azure with Managed Identity?
followUpsQA.a: Assign only the specific RBAC role the service needs on the specific resource - for example, Key Vault Secrets User on one vault, not Key Vault Administrator on the subscription.

followUpsQA.q: What does deny by default mean in practice?
followUpsQA.a: Start with no access and grant only what is explicitly needed - the opposite of granting broad access and trying to lock down exceptions later.

followUpsQA.q: How often should you review access grants?
followUpsQA.a: At least quarterly and whenever an employee changes role, leaves, or a service is decommissioned - unused access is a standing attack surface.

followUpsQA.q: Does least privilege apply to API tokens and OAuth scopes?
followUpsQA.a: Yes - request only the OAuth scopes the application actually needs. An app that reads a user's profile should request the profile scope, not the admin or write scope.

dialogue.text: Define the principle of least privilege and tell me how you apply it in a .NET application.

Least privilege means every user, service, and process gets only the minimum access it needs to do its specific job. The goal is to limit blast radius - if something gets compromised, it can reach as little as possible.
Concrete example - how does this apply to a database connection in a .NET app?

The application's database account should have only SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE on the tables it actually uses - not db_owner. If a SQL injection attack succeeds against that account, it cannot drop tables, read other schemas, or execute stored procedures it has no business calling.
What about access to Azure resources like Key Vault or Storage?

Scope the Managed Identity's RBAC role to the specific resource. An ordering service that reads two Key Vault secrets should get Key Vault Secrets User on that specific vault, not Contributor on the entire resource group or subscription.
An app is compromised. Walk me through how least privilege changes the outcome.

An attacker who controls the app process can only do what the process's identity is permitted to do. With db_owner they can drop every table and read every schema. With a limited account they can access only the Orders and Products tables with specific operations - the attack is contained to the scope of that identity.
How do you make sure access does not drift over time as the team grows?

Periodic access reviews at least quarterly and triggered by role changes or departures. In Azure, Privileged Identity Management enforces just-in-time access for admin roles. For database permissions I use Infrastructure as Code so they are explicit and auditable rather than manual grants that accumulate silently.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

questions.q: How would you respond if you discovered a secret had been committed to the repository?

answer.oneLiner: Treat the secret as already compromised: rotate or revoke it immediately, then remove it from git history, and add scanning to prevent it happening again.
answer.detail: The moment a secret is committed to a repository it is potentially compromised - this is true even for private repositories, because they can be cloned, forked, accessed by CI/CD systems, or breached. Simply deleting the file or committing a blank replacement is not enough because the secret still exists in git history and on every clone. The response has two phases. Immediate: rotate or revoke the secret with the issuing service (the old key is now dead regardless of who has it), and notify the relevant service owner. Cleanup: remove the secret from git history using git filter-repo (the recommended modern tool), force-push to the remote, and notify all collaborators to re-clone because their local histories still contain it. Prevention going forward: add secret scanning to the repository (GitHub Secret Scanning, GitLeaks, or truffleHog), add a pre-commit hook that checks for common secret patterns, and move secrets to Key Vault so they are never in files in the first place.
keyPoints: Rotate or revoke the secret first - assume it is compromised, whether the repo is private or public
Deleting the file or blanking it in a new commit is not enough - the secret lives in git history
Remove from history with git filter-repo and notify all collaborators to re-clone
Add secret scanning (GitHub Secret Scanning or GitLeaks) and pre-commit hooks to prevent recurrence
Move secrets to Key Vault so they are never committed to the repo in the first place

code.note: Configuration binds to IOptions<StripeOptions> in business logic - the source (Key Vault, env var, user-secrets) is transparent to the consuming code.

answer.analogy: A committed secret is a lost house key - changing the lock immediately matters more than searching for the key.
redFlags: Saying delete the file and commit again - the secret remains in git history on every clone and fork
Assuming a private repository makes a committed secret safe - private repos get cloned, breached, and accessed by CI runners
Not knowing that rotation must happen before the history cleanup - cleanup without rotation leaves the active secret exposed
Not knowing tools like git filter-repo, GitHub Secret Scanning, or GitLeaks for incident response

followUpsQA.q: Why is deleting the file in a new commit not sufficient?
followUpsQA.a: The secret still exists in every prior commit in git history, and on every clone and fork - it is recoverable by anyone with repo access until it is rotated.

followUpsQA.q: What tool do you use to remove a file from git history?
followUpsQA.a: git filter-repo is the current recommended tool - it rewrites history to remove the file from all commits, then you force-push and all collaborators must re-clone.

followUpsQA.q: Does having a private repo mean you can skip the rotation step?
followUpsQA.a: No - private repos are cloned by developers, CI runners, and deployment tools, and can be breached. The only safe assumption is that the secret is compromised.

followUpsQA.q: What scanning tools prevent secrets being committed in the first place?
followUpsQA.a: GitHub Secret Scanning detects known secret patterns on push. GitLeaks or truffleHog run as pre-commit hooks to catch secrets before they are committed.

followUpsQA.q: What is the correct order of steps when you discover the incident?
followUpsQA.a: Rotate first to neutralize the active secret, then clean git history, notify collaborators to re-clone, then add scanning to prevent recurrence.

dialogue.text: You are doing a code review and you spot that a production Stripe API key was committed to the repository three commits ago. What do you do?
First thing: rotate or revoke the key with Stripe immediately. The old key is now dead regardless of who has it - that is the priority. I would not wait for the history cleanup before doing this.
Can I just delete the file and commit the fix? That removes it from the repo.

No - that removes it from the current state but the secret still exists in every previous commit in git history, and on every developer machine that cloned the repo. Anyone with repo access can check out that old commit and read it.
How do you actually remove it from history?

Use git filter-repo to rewrite history and remove the file from all commits, then force-push to the remote. After that I notify all collaborators that they must re-clone - their local histories still contain the secret and a simple pull will not fix that.
This was in a private repository only the team can access. Does that change the urgency?

No - private repos are accessed by CI runners, deployment pipelines, developer machines, and third-party integrations, all of which can be compromised. The safe assumption is always that the secret is exposed. Rotation costs minutes - a breach costs much more.
How do you make sure this never happens again?

Three layers: enable GitHub Secret Scanning so pushes containing known secret patterns are blocked at the remote; add GitLeaks as a pre-commit hook so secrets are caught before they are committed; and move all secrets to Azure Key Vault so developers never have them in local files in the first place.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅
